data.title: Gold At $5,000 - So What Happens Next?
data.description:
Gold is on a historic run. After hitting more than 50 new all-time highs last year, the yellow metal has surged to a new all-time high above $5,000 an ounce, a once-unthinkable amount. It's now doubled in value since September 2024.

Meanwhile, silver, the "poor man's gold," is not so poor any longer, having smashed through $110 an ounce.

As for where we go from here, analysts are making some bold forecasts. Goldman Sachs just raised its year-end gold price target to $5,400 an ounce, citing strong demand from both institutional and retail buyers, and the London Bullion Market Association's (LBMA) most recent survey reports bullish forecasts as high as $7,150.

If you recall, back in September, I projected that gold could hit $7,000 by the end of President Trump's second term on the growing mountain of debt and a cornered Federal Reserve, and I still stand by that projection.

Is this the Perfect Storm?

As I see it, the surge in gold prices is the result of multiple tailwinds converging all at once, including monetary, fiscal, geopolitical, and even psychological.

For one, we continue to have runaway government spending and ballooning national debt. The U.S. is on track to add trillions in new deficits over the next few years, and with debt levels approaching 125% of GDP, the government can't afford significantly higher interest rates.

This puts the Fed between a rock and a hard place. Raising rates would risk a fiscal crisis, while cutting rates would risk an even weaker dollar, which is down about 10% since the start of Trump 2.0.

And speaking of the dollar, there's a growing crisis of confidence in fiat currencies and central banks, especially the Fed. More on that later.

And finally, central banks around the world are buying gold at a historic pace. China, India, Turkey, and others are stockpiling bullion and diversifying away from the dollar and U.S. Treasuries.

Fed Independence Under Fire

One of the most underappreciated forces driving gold's meteoric rise right now is, I believe, the growing concern about the independence of the Fed.

President Trump has made no secret of his frustration with Fed Chair Jerome Powell. This year, that frustration has escalated into something more serious and potentially dangerous for the credibility of monetary policy.

In an unprecedented move, the Justice Department issued a criminal subpoena to the Fed, targeting Powell over alleged cost overruns in building renovations. The Fed chairman responded with a rare video statement, warning that the investigation could be a pretext to force rate cuts.

To my knowledge, this is the first time a sitting Fed Chair has essentially accused the White House of trying to strong-arm monetary policy.

This should raise some red flags. The whole point of an independent central bank is to ensure long-term economic stability, even when it's politically inconvenient. When that independence is undermined, markets take note, as they are now.

Turkey's Cautionary Tale

To understand where this road leads, look no further than Turkey.

Turkish President Recep Erdoğan has systemically undermined his country's central bank over the past decade, firing governors who refused to cut interest rates and replacing them with political loyalists. He embraced the unorthodox belief that higher, rather than lower, rates cause inflation, a theory that runs counter to centuries of economic history.

The result? Turkey's inflation soared past 80%, the lira collapsed against the dollar, and the economy teetered on the edge of catastrophe. Only after the damage was done did Erdoğan finally reverse course, bringing in traditional leadership and allowing rates to be hiked (to exorbitant levels).

Like China and India, Turkey has a deep-seated affinity for gold, and when inflation began to rip, many Turkish investors increased their purchases of the metal as a hedge. It all added up! In October, when gold surpassed $4,000, the country's central bank estimated that households' total gold holdings had surged to $500 billion.

A Crisis of Confidence in the Dollar

It's not just Turkey, though, and it's not just individual investors.

Central banks from China to Switzerland have been steadily increasing their gold reserves. At the same time, they're scaling back exposure to U.S. Treasuries and reducing dollar holdings.

This global diversification away from the greenback is part of a broader "debasement trade," a strategy rooted in the belief that the dollar, like other fiat currencies, is losing its long-term value.

Gold has already surpassed U.S. Treasurys as a percent of total global foreign reserves. Is the dollar next?

Gold Stocks Still Have Room to Run

What if you want exposure to gold but don't want the hassle of storing it? If you've been following my work over the years, you know I'm a big believer in gold mining stocks as a leveraged play on rising gold prices.

These companies tend to move two to three times faster than the metal itself-both on the upside and downside.

But what's different about this cycle is that many miners have cleaned up their balance sheets, reduced debt, and focused on shareholder returns. That means they're in a stronger position than in past gold bull markets.

Also worth watching are royalty and streaming companies, like Wheaton Precious Metals, Franco-Nevada, and OR Royalties (formerly Osisko). These firms generate revenue based on production and price, without the same operational risks as traditional miners. OR, for example, just posted 100% earnings growth, and analysts are forecasting more upside ahead.

Stay the Course

If you've been investing in gold or gold stocks, congratulations! You've been on the right side of history.

If you're still on the sidelines, I wouldn't wait for $6,000 to start participating.

We're living through a period of profound global monetary transition. The cracks are starting to show in the fiat currency system, and investors are waking up to the need for real assets in their portfolios.

As I said back in September, $7,000 gold is no fantasy. I believe it's a logical destination in a world where confidence in central banks is eroding, debts are exploding and governments are increasingly reaching for control.

data.title: Waste Management: Growth Inflection Ahead As Volume Headwinds Fade, Pricing Stays Strong
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Waste Management's (WM) organic revenue growth should accelerate in 2026 as industrial activity recovers with a more supportive interest rate environment, residential headwinds ease as the large franchise loss that impacted its results in 2025 is lapped, healthcare Solutions business stabilizes and returns to growth, and RNG production continues to ramp. The company's medium- to long-term growth prospects are also attractive, with strong pricing power supported by landfill scarcity, and cross-selling opportunities in the healthcare business.

The company's margins should benefit from positive price-cost spread, automation and process improvement, RNG volumes ramping up, and normalization in the healthcare business. While the company's earnings and cash flow growth prospects are improving, the stock is trading at a discount to historical and peer valuations. I find the risk-reward attractive and hence rate it a Buy.

Recent Financial Performance

I last covered WM stock in June, and the stock has slightly declined since then. While the company's legacy business has performed well, the company has seen some ERP-related issues in the healthcare business, where there have been instances like incorrect billings, due to which the company has to delay some price increases and provide one-time credits to its customers. In Q3 2025, Waste Management reported revenue of about $6.44 billion, representing 14.9% year-over-year growth in revenues. While organic revenue increased 3.7% year-over-year, Healthcare Solutions added up approximately $628 million. Pricing was the main driver for organic growth, with collection and disposal core pricing up 6% year-over-year and yield up 3.8% year-over-year. Volumes in collection and disposal business were slightly positive at around 0.2% YoY. Volumes benefited from strong landfill demand and improving industrial activity, but they were partially offset by continued decline in residential collection volumes as the company exited lower margin business. The industrial collection volumes were up 1.2% year-over-year, marking the first positive quarter since 2022 and indicating early signs of cyclical recovery. Landfill volumes increased 5.2% year-over-year, with municipal solid waste up 5% year-over-year and special waste up 5.5% year-over-year. This increase was largely unrelated to wildfire cleanup activity, which had benefited prior quarters, indicating that underlying demand is improving.

On the margin front, total company adjusted EBITDA margin reached 30.6%, a modest improvement vs. 30.5% in Q3 last year. The legacy Waste Management business showed strong operating leverage with adjusted EBITDA margin expanding to 32%, up 150 basis points year over year. However, the consolidation of lower-margin healthcare business, where existing margins were just 17.5%, negatively impacted the overall results.

Outlook

Moving forward, I'm optimistic about Waste Management's growth prospects. The headwinds that impacted the business over the last couple of years are now fading. The industrial volumes, which have been negative for the last several quarters, are now inflecting. With the interest rate cycle now starting to reverse, I expect industrial production to pick up, which should result in higher waste generation from this market and continue to help Waste Management's volume.

Residential volumes were a big negative in 2025, as the company continued to shed low-margin work. The loss of a major franchise contract alone created a 250 basis point headwind for the residential volumes. As this impact is lapped in 2026, we should see a significant reduction in the reported decline rate.

Pricing should continue to remain strong moving forward, helping both revenues and margins. Landfill scarcity and regulatory barriers to new capacity should continue to help pricing. Management has also shown a good pricing discipline and has guided for positive price/cost moving forward as well, which should help both revenues and margins. Given the strategic location of the company's landfill assets around major population centers and the scarcity of landfill capacity due to tough permit requirements, I expect the company can continue to charge prices ahead of cost inflation. This should support both near and long-term revenue and earnings prospects.

Beyond the core waste franchise, the company should also benefit from a ramp-up in its renewable production. The company has doubled its renewable natural gas production in the first nine months of 2025. On its last earnings call, management also noted that 45% of 2026 offtake is already pre-sold. This indicates strong demand for the company's offerings. Over the last few years, the company has invested heavily in building capacity in this business. As the production continues to ramp, it should nicely add to the company's EBITDA. Further, since significant CapEx has already been done over the past few years in this business, and the CapEx requirements moving forward are expected to normalize, this should help improve free cash flow as well.

Healthcare is another major growth opportunity. The company is focusing on improving pricing in this business and cross-selling with legacy business. On its last earnings call, management noted that more than 7,000 small to mid-sized customers have already been cross-sold, and one large hospital customer expanded its annual spend by $5 million across its multi-state network. In addition, approximately $200 million of customer contracts have been renewed at low double-digit price increases. As the company continues to focus on pricing increases and cross-selling, this segment should see good growth in the medium to long term. There are some near-term issues as the company transitions the healthcare business ERP onto the core platform, which are impacting revenues and margins. However, management expects these issues to stabilize by the end of Q1 2026. So I expect a more consistent performance as the year progresses.

I am also optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. As discussed, the price-cost is expected to remain positive moving forward as well, which should help margins even in a low volume growth environment. The company is also doing a good job in terms of lowering its operating costs. Waste Management is improving efficiency across the network through technology deployment and process optimization, and implementing initiatives like routing optimization, real-time scheduling, and using productivity tools to lower costs. This should continue to help margins.

In addition, margins should also benefit from improving business mix. As the company continues to shed low-margin residential contracts, the overall margin mix should improve. When we combine lower costs and an improving mix with a potential recovery in the core collection and disposable volume, with industrial production improving and residential becoming less negative, it should result in a nice operating leverage in the core business.

Further, in the recycling business, the company is doing a good job in terms of modernizing and automating its plants. This should help its margins. The renewable business is also ramping up after significant investment over the last few years, which should add nicely to margins and cash flow. Also, as discussed earlier, while the healthcare business faces some temporary ERP issues-related headwinds, once these headwinds fade post Q1 2026, the margin in this business should also improve in the second half of FY26 and beyond.

Overall, I am optimistic about the company's revenue as well as margin prospects driven by strong pricing, industrial volumes recovering, residential headwinds fading, renewable production ramping, productivity initiatives and automation, and ERP issues in the healthcare business normalizing.

Q4 Earnings Preview

Waste Management is set to report its Q4 earnings on January 28 after market close. Last quarter, the company offered outsized credit for healthcare clients as it tried to clear up past due account receivables. These were one-time credits, and I don't expect them to repeat in Q4. So, I am expecting better performance in the healthcare business, which should allay some investor concerns. Also, the company is starting to lap large franchisee losses at the end of Q4, so residential performance should also start improving. Overall, I am expecting better performance from the core collection and disposable business and healthcare segment. On the other hand, I am expecting softer results from the recycling business, mostly due to pricing pressure. If we look at corrugated recycled paper prices, they have been trending down. However, I am not too worried about them, as their price decline is well known and already reflected in consensus estimates. Further, given OCC prices are much closer to trough levels compared to historical peaks, indicates we can see potential recovery moving forward. Overall, I am optimistic about quarterly results and would be watching closely the commentary around core collection and disposable business as well as healthcare trends.

Valuation

Waste Management is trading at 27.15x FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $8.34 and 23.82x FY27 consensus EPS estimate of $9.51.

This is a discount vs its 5-year average forward P/E of 28.84x. The stock is also trading at a discount to its peers. Republic Services (RSG) is trading at 29.35x FY26 consensus EPS estimate and 26.36x FY27 consensus EPS estimate, while Waste Connections (WCN) is trading at 29.54x FY26 consensus EPS estimate and 26.27x FY27 consensus EPS estimate. So, the stock is attractively valued vs. its historical level as well as its peers.

I believe several headwinds that have impacted the company's results in the past and impacted its valuation multiples are now fading. Industrial volumes have already turned positive, and with the interest rate cycle reversing, I expect the recovery to continue. The company is set to lap the residential headwind from the loss of a major franchise, which created a 250 basis point drag in 2025. This should also help volumes in 2026. The ERP integration issues in the healthcare business, which have resulted in short-term revenue and margin impact, are also expected to stabilize post Q1 FY26, which should help the results in the back half and future years.

Further, the renewable business is also ramping up after an investment-heavy phase of capacity building over the last few years. This should not only help revenues and margins but also improve FCF due to less capex requirement moving forward. Management has guided for an FCF of around $3.8 billion in FY26, compared to $2.8 to $2.9 billion in FCF guidance for the last year. This should improve the overall capital return profile of the business. The company is expected to post double-digit EPS growth in FY26 and FY27. This, coupled with a decent 1.5% dividend yield plus prospects of valuation re-rating as the results improve, should result in a good upside for the stock. Overall, I find the risk-reward attractive with good growth prospects, and the stock trading at a discount to its historical levels and peers. Hence, I have a buy rating on WM.

Risks

Management has indicated that ERP issues in the healthcare business should stabilize by Q1 2026. However, if there are further delays and prolonged revenue and margin issues in this business, it may weigh on investor sentiment and pressure the stock.

I am expecting a recovery in industrial volumes as the interest rate cycle turns and the Federal Reserve continues to decrease interest rates. However, if rate cuts are delayed or are smaller than expected, industrial volumes could remain subdued for longer and impact overall earnings growth.

Takeaway

Waste Management is positioned for good near-term and long-term growth. In the near term, the company should benefit from industrial activity recovering, residential headwinds easing, healthcare solutions stabilizing, and the ramp-up of renewable gas production. In the longer term, the structural pricing power, cross-sell synergies in healthcare, automation-driven cost savings, and higher contribution from renewables should support revenue and margin growth. The stock looks attractive, trading at a discount vs. historical valuation and peers. I find risk-reward attractive and have a buy rating on this stock.

data.title: XAU/USD And Metals Outlook: Gold Pushes To Pre-FOMC Record, Holding The Metals Market
data.description:
By Elior Manier

A lot has changed since our last high-timeframe outlook, reminding us that black swan events can affect any projections and expectations in a matter of a headline and a session.

The start of 2026 has been marked by chaotic headlines and geopolitical events, which is now a new norm. Some call it the New World Order (or New World Disorder).

Last weekend offered quite a show across markets and journals as the US president decided to threaten additional tariffs to European nations. Donald Trump reaffirmed his desire to acquire Greenland, sparking widespread concern ahead of the World Economic Conference, held in Davos last week.

The immediate reactions were flash US dollar selloffs and metals extending much higher in a newfound risk-off move. Despite the headlines materializing into something much less concerning for the world as we know it, the confidence damage from the latest TACO has prolonged the damage to the Greenback and supported the precious commodities.

About a week after the headlines, silver reached the $100 milestone while gold accelerated to $5,000, not even mentioning the swift rises in platinum, palladium, and other metals.

Shortly after reaching the new milestones, however, some divergences between metals have emerged and are turning into quite sudden reversals. Silver topped suddenly at $117 after a 27% weekly explosion, platinum spiked at $2,884 and now stands 12% lower, while gold holds solidly above $5,000.

Ahead of FOMC events, moves can be exaggerated by lower volumes and orders, which can push supply and demand to have a more significant impact on prices – a result of the past two days of action in commodities and the US dollar.

The most extensive tests for the run in metals are about to unfold, both regarding the Federal Reserve:

  • The first is tomorrow's monthly FOMC meeting (the first of eight in 2026) – a cut is far from priced in (only 3%), so participants will be listening very closely to what Powell says during his press conference.
  • The second, less predictable, is the nomination for the next Fed Chair – Powell's term as Chair ends in May 2026 (but will be able to remain at the Board of Governors).
  • Rick Rieder is now the favorite, not far in front of Kevin Warsh – this only looks at the current prediction market odds.
  • Some geopolitical events could still largely change the picture.

Let's dive right into a technical analysis for gold (XAU/USD), silver (XAG/USD) and platinum (XPT/USD) to spot where the current trend stands, just ahead of the FOMC meeting.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart and Technical Levels

Gold easily brushed through any pessimistic outlook as the geopolitical tone worsened yet again. After remaining dormant in the first few trading days of 2026, the bullion managed a swift push to daily new records, forming a technical tight bull channel.

Last week's push was consistent, backed by the changing fundamental environment. As other metals now struggle, XAU is showing why it's the ultimate safe haven: As its peers are giving up their high gains, it remains strongly above $5,000 (and nearing $5,100 as we speak).

A potential US intervention in Iran is still looming.

Tomorrow's FOMC meeting will have a high chance to affect the current flows.

  • A neutral/hawkish Fed (base case) could reduce demand, which may prompt a correction to at least $4,600.
  • A dovish Fed (less likely) would pump gold to swift new highs (a quick test of potential resistance around $5,300).
    • The same would follow in other metals.

In terms of Fed nominees, some analysts argue that Rick Rieder would be bearish on metals (bullish on bonds and the USD), while Kevin Warsh would keep the trend as it is.

Higher timeframe levels to watch for gold (XAU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $5,000 to $5,100 major psychological resistance
  • $5,115 all-time highs and running
  • Key Fibonacci projection $5,250 to $5,350
  • Next psychological level at $5,500

Support Levels:

  • Preceding ATH Pivot $4,400 to $4,500 – bullish above, bearish below
  • Channel break-retest around $4,800 (+/- $30)
  • 20-Day moving average: $4,635
  • Pivotal support and channel lows $3,880 to $4,050
  • $3,200 to $3,500 major support

Silver (XAG/USD) Daily Chart and Technical Levels

The ongoing parabolic ascension remains historic as the devil's metal elevates to new record highs, up at a whopping 27% since January 16! A $10 move is now the new normal in the ongoing reckless squeeze – a note to take into account in case volatility keeps rising from here.

The metal reached $117, just shy of $120. After reaching the new record, a flash sale took silver to a retest of the $102 level. Shorter timeframes indicate a triangle consolidation as the pre-FOMC action looks undecided.

  • Breaking below its support could point to a swifter retracement, with the $93 to $95 being a reasonable target – a larger retracement could, of course, occur (watch out for mean reversion in such violent markets).
  • Any push to a new record could easily take the metal to the $125 psychological level.

Higher timeframe levels to watch for silver (XAG/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $114 to $117 current ATH resistance
  • Current record: $117.75
  • $125.00 next psychological resistance

Support Levels:

  • $100 psychological level
  • $93.50 to $96.00 Jan 20 highs – current momentum pivot
  • Mini support $83 to $85
  • Minor support $70 to $75 above bullish, bearish below
  • Christmas lows $70
  • Pivotal support $48 to $50

Platinum (XPT/USD) Daily Chart and Technical Levels

Platinum looks poised for a test of its upward channel lower bound around $2,350 to $2,390 after stalling its rise suddenly. Bears appeared in the metal quickly after gold reached $5,000, while XPT/USD wicked at $2,882.

With the current action looking more like a consolidation/slight correction, it will be very interesting to see if bulls manage to retake the upper hand after the FOMC.

  • A daily close above $2,695 points to new record highs.
  • Failing to do so may trigger a sharper correction in the precious metal - look at its 20-day moving average ($2,353).

data.title: Stellantis: Why A Rebound Is Closer Than You Think
data.description:
Legacy automaker Stellantis N.V. (STLA) has had a rough couple of years, especially in North America. This has been mainly due to:

  1. Inventory glut
  2. Leadership changes
  3. Falling profit margins

This has, understandably, caused its share price to collapse to post-merger lows during the past 12 months. However, I believe that the company can actually turn things around going forward and am giving the stock a Buy rating for the following reasons:

  1. Inventory normalization
  2. A refocus of its product line
  3. Factorial solid-state batteries
  4. The EU and Mercosur Trade Agreement

What's important about these catalysts is that they range from short-term changes that can be made quickly to longer-term ones that give the company a larger runway for growth.

The Short-Term Catalysts

To begin with, there are two short-term catalysts that could help the company recover sooner than some may think. The first is inventory. As readers are likely aware at this point in the article, Stellantis is facing an oversupply of vehicles. But why did this happen?

Well, under previous CEO Carlos Tavares, Stellantis had chosen to double down on higher-end vehicles in order to achieve double-digit profit margins. However, this backfired not only because these expensive models were low-volume sellers, but because the company also decided to phase out high-volume models that were appealing to value-conscious consumers. As a result, many would-be customers ended up purchasing from other brands, and the inventory buildup, ironically, ended up hurting profit margins.

However, management took steps to reduce inventory bloat, as CEO Antonio Filosa, who took over in May 2025, slashed prices to move vehicles. By bringing the inventory down to more normal levels, Stellantis now has the ability to set prices that are in line with its profit goals and reduce discounts. This will boost the company's margins, which should help push its stock price higher. In addition, better inventory levels now give the firm the ability to turn its attention to growth, which leads us to the next catalyst: giving the consumers what they actually want.

If Stellantis wants to regain market share (market share dropped from 12.5% in 2020 to 8% by the end of 2025), then the company must produce the vehicles that buyers are willing to pay for. Two models that the company is placing its hopes on are the Ram Hemi V8 and a new Jeep Cherokee. This is because full-sized pickups and SUVs continue to dominate sales in the U.S. and offer higher margins. And since these have been core offerings for a long time, they could help boost earnings quickly.

In fact, by simply starting to move back to what consumers want, the firm saw its first quarter of revenue growth in Q3 after six consecutive quarters of decline. More precisely, revenue grew by 13% to €37.2 billion. This rebound was mainly driven by a recovery in U.S. sales led by Jeep's Wrangler, Gladiator, and Wagoneer models.

In addition, it looks like this growth trend will continue into Q4. Indeed, FCA US LLC, Stellantis' North American subsidiary that designs, manufactures, and sells or distributes vehicles under the Chrysler, Dodge, Jeep, Ram, FIAT, and Alfa Romeo brands, recently released Q4 sales numbers. Importantly, U.S. sales grew by 4% year-over-year, which made it the second consecutive quarter of growth. The subsidiary even stated that it grew its market share, although it didn't specify by how much. If we assume that Stellantis' overall sales will follow a similar pattern, then the company should be in good shape.

It's also worth noting that most U.S. consumers still prefer to buy gas or diesel vehicles over electric vehicles. More specifically, 61% of consumers are planning to buy gas or diesel vehicles for their next car. At the same time, hybrids and plug-in hybrids together make up 26% of purchase intent, while battery electric vehicles make up only 7%.

The reasons for this are unsurprising. Electric vehicles have higher upfront costs and weaker resale values. Moreover, 47% of buyers point to range anxiety as a reason why they prefer gas-powered cars, while 44% cite the time it takes to charge. Therefore, it makes more sense for Stellantis to focus on popular gas-powered pickups and SUVs, which, based on FCA US LLC's results, seems to be working.

The Long-Term Catalyst

Although Stellantis is shifting its focus back to internal combustion engines, it is not abandoning electric vehicle technology altogether. In 2026, the company plans to launch a test fleet of Dodge Charger Daytona vehicles with Factorial solid-state batteries to evaluate their performance in real-world environments.

This is an interesting development and long-term catalyst because these batteries promise much faster charging than today's lithium-ion batteries due to their higher energy density of above 375 Wh/kg. More precisely, they can charge from 15% to 90% in about 18 minutes. Given that 44% of buyers don't like how long it takes for electric vehicles to charge, being able to reduce the time could give it a huge advantage going forward.

It also shows that the company is getting ready for potential changes in electric vehicle preferences without heavily impacting profitability in the short to medium term, which could help with investor sentiment, as it would suggest that management is competent.

This argument can be further supported by Stellantis' flexible "multi-energy" platforms that are designed to build gasoline, hybrid, and electric versions of the same vehicle on a single production line. This will allow the firm to adjust electric and gas-powered vehicle output based on real-time consumer demand, as well as reduce capital risk and ultimately improve cash flow.

Another long-term catalyst that has significant long-term potential is the European Union's new trade deal with Mercosur (Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay, and Paraguay), which includes a gradual removal of the 35% auto and 14-18% parts tariffs.

Since Stellantis has operations in both Europe and South America, this makes it cheaper for the company to export vehicles and components from its European plants in Italy, France, and Germany. Interestingly, the European Commission estimates that auto exports to these countries could eventually triple by 2040, which would give Stellantis a long runway for profitable growth.

This deal also helps Stellantis prepare better for an eventual transition into more electric vehicles because Mercosur countries are rich in raw materials that are essential for batteries, such as lithium, graphite, and manganese. Therefore, European companies will be able to have easier and cheaper access to these materials while reducing their reliance on China.

There Are Still Risks

Despite the catalysts above, there are still risks that investors need to be aware of. Beginning with the Mercosur deal, the benefits won't appear overnight. In fact, tariffs will be phased out over the next 10 to 15 years in order to give local industries enough time to adjust to European competition. On top of that, the EU-Mercosur deal still needs to pass a legal review by the European Court of Justice, which could delay things by up to two years.

Separately, there's always the possibility that consumers may not respond as well as expected to the new ICE-powered models that Stellantis is focusing on. This is especially true if gas prices suddenly spike, since it would make EVs more appealing.

In such a scenario, inventory would once again increase, lead to more discounts, and hurt margins. Furthermore, although the solid-state battery test fleet sounds promising, the technology is still in its early stages. It also doesn't help that these types of batteries are expensive and tricky to scale commercially.

Lastly, the auto sector is highly cyclical, and a macroeconomic slowdown will hit the entire industry especially hard. Therefore, investors need to keep a close eye on the overall economy.

Nevertheless, I believe that management will be able to execute on its turnaround plan, which will boost profits in the near-term while the company builds on its longer-term catalysts.

Valuation

When it comes to valuation, we can actually keep it easy for Stellantis. The stock is currently trading at a price-to-book value of 0.33x, which is near the lowest valuation point in its history. As a result, I believe the catalysts mentioned above could eventually help the stock reach a P/B ratio of 1.05x, which was the case when the stock peaked in 2024.

The Takeaway

Stellantis has had a rough couple of years due to strategic errors that led to an oversupply of inventory and lower profit margins. However, CEO Filosa seems to be in touch with consumer preferences, and the company's approach to preparing for electric vehicles without overcommitting makes me bullish on the stock.

data.title: Apple: Rolling Over Into Key Earnings
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) heads into the big holiday quarter report with the stock finally rolling over. The tech giant still lacks an AI business, and the expected launch of the AI Siri comes at a big cost, not a new revenue stream. My investment thesis remains ultra-bearish on Apple trading at a premium valuation with no signs of strong growth ahead.

Costly AI

Apple investors have long seen AI as an opportunity for Apple to generate a revenue stream similar to the Google partnership on Internet search. Google is paying Apple upwards of $20 billion for exclusive search placement in iOS products, but the tech giant has to pay for generative AI tech.

The company has reportedly chosen to work with Gemini from Google (GOOGL) at a reported cost of $1 billion. The news suggests Anthropic (ANTHRO) wanted $2+ billion over multiple years for working with Siri.

So Apple enters 2026 with no working AI solution yet and no real plans to monetize AI other than selling more iPhones. The exciting product news going into Fiscal Q1 '26 earnings on Thursday, January 29th, is the new AirTag product that sells for only $29.

Apple will apparently use Google Gemini to build the Apple Foundation Model to be utilized by Siri. Since Apple has already launched Apple Intelligence and has made this part of the basic iPhone software package for Siri, the tech giant doesn't appear to have a way to directly monetize AI.

The company plans to release the more personalized Siri voice assistant at the end of February. The recent stock dip from an all-time high of $288 is likely due to the final realization Apple doesn't have a method to monetize AI, outside of search via Google.

Big FQ1

With the holidays over, the focus will shift to the FQ1 numbers released on Thursday after the market closes. The consensus analyst estimates are as follows:

  • EPS of $2.67, up 11.3% YoY.
  • Revenue of $138.5 billion, up 11.4% YoY.

The big focus of the earnings call will be whether Apple has any ability to sustain growth in the double-digit range going forward. The market hasn't really explained the iPhone 17 sales boost with the new iPhone Air failing to drive growth and the hot iPhone 17 Pro version lacking any real AI in Apple Intelligence.

Most notably, IDC has predicted iPhone sales in 2025 of 247.4 million units, highest level on record. The amount tops the record of 236 million units back in 2021, suggesting Apple benefitted more from an upgrade cycle after 4 years than any real strong performance from a hot new AI product.

In fact, IDC appears to suggest iOS smartphone sales will dip next year due to the push out of the next iPhone base model from 2026 to early 2027 along with memory supply shortages. The forecast is for a sharp decline of 4.2% units followed by a 5.4% rebound in 2027, leading to the only revenue growth potential from iPhones the next 2 years due to higher ASPs.

For this reason, the expectation is for Apple to watch sales dip back to the mid-single digits going forward, similar to consensus analyst forecasts. The company is forecast to grow FY27 revenues by only 6.7%, and the EPS target is forecast to grow 10.6% to $9.14.

The whole world is currently in an investment cycle spending on AI, yet Apple has just focused on leveraging the business up via squeezing more profits out of the dated iPhone.

Apple faces a much bigger threat, as Meta Platforms (META) has a viable smart glasses product. Meta is already working with the Ray-Ban owner to boost smart glasses production to 20 to 30 million units this year in the first real product that is a potential future replacement for both the iPhone and the Apple Watch.

Even with the recent pullback, the stock trades at 27x FY27 EPS targets. As well, Apple requires upwards of $100 billion in aggressive annual share buybacks in order to achieve the EPS boost above the revenue growth. With a $3.65 trillion market cap, the $110 billion buyback from FY25 provides a nearly 3% boost to EPS.

Apple is busy spending every dollar of free cash flow from operations (only produced $111B in CFO in FY25) to boost short-term earnings growth, while Anthropic has already built an AI business with $9 billion in ARR. The generative AI company is looking for a fundraising of $350 billion, highlighting the opportunity Apple has missed out on.

The tech giant would be better suited to invest in AI and/or smart glasses technology than trying to maximize profits. As discussed in prior research, Apple should trade around 15x forward EPS targets with the forecast for a return to 5% to 6% sales growth, placing the stock at a valuation of ~$137.

While Apple recently hit all-time highs, the stock has actually underperformed the market over the last 5 years. This underperformance is likely to persist with no real AI monetization and a stock trading at a premium valuation of nearly 3x the growth rate while spending virtually all free cash flow on share buybacks at an inflated stock price.

Apple has a large installed user base, so the company could still figure out how to monetize the user base with AI. The Services division is still growing at a 15% annual clip, so the tech giant still has a path to growth with or without an AI boost.

Also, Apple has AR/VR technology and plans for smart glasses. The company could come through with new technology to upset the current first mover advantage of Meta with expectations for Apple Glasses in 2026, though likely to slip to 2027.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Apple is set to report a solid FQ1. The big question is whether the rebounding growth rate is sustainable, and all signs suggest this isn't the case.

Investors should've used the recent run to all-time highs to cash out since the current price is still very expensive for a company not even participating in the growth area of the next decade.

data.title: VFQY And VFMF: Quality Vs. Multifactor Exposure
data.description:
In this article, I look at two U.S. factor-based ETFs from Vanguard. The Vanguard U.S. Quality Factor ETF Shares (VFQY) and the Vanguard U.S. Multifactor ETF Shares (VFMF). My objective here is to determine the benefits provided by the quality screening process (in terms of VFQY) and compare it to the broader multifactor approach (in terms of VFMF).

My investment thesis is the following: Based on current sector tilts facing mixed headwinds, and both funds having failed to protect against the downside during multiple recent major market selloffs, I rate VFQY and VFMF a ‘Hold’.

While valuation discounts exist for both funds, historical underperformance compared to the benchmark and lack of any clear catalysts leave me remaining cautious.

Overview of the Funds

Both VFQY and VFMF have an inception dating back to February 2018, providing approximately 8 years of performance and fund management to consider. According to the funds’ website, the objective of these funds is the following:

“[The fund(s) seek long-term capital appreciation by investing in] a diverse mix of stocks representing many different market capitalizations (large, mid, and small), market sectors, and industry groups.” – Prospectuses for VFQY and VFMF

Furthermore, having been around since 1975, Vanguard is one of the top investment management firms in the world.

The Investment Screening Processes

In terms of the investment screening process, VFQY uses a rules-based quantitative model to evaluate stocks. This process begins with a broad universe of US equities, which is then reduced based on numerical rankings considering fundamental factors.

Specifically, the fund aims to target firms with strong underlying fundamentals, meaning strong profitability and healthy balance sheets. The quality factor (as outlined in the fund’s prospectus) is determined by operating profitability, gross profitability, change in net operating assets, intangibles, and equity issuance. Firms with excessive levels of debt, volatile earnings, and weak/deteriorating fundamentals are penalized in the process. The Russell 3000 Index, as represented by the Vanguard Russell 3000 Index Fund ETF Shares (VTHR), is used as the underlying benchmark, and the fund is reconstituted annually.

For VFMF, the process again begins with a broad universe of US stocks. This universe is reduced based on factors such as value, momentum, quality, and price stability. A weighting process is used to allocate across several factors, meaning that firms must score well in multiple factors to meet the selection criteria. Similar to VFQY, VFMF invests in at least 80% US equities, utilizes the Russell 3000 as the underlying benchmark, and is reconstituted annually.

Where VFQY specifically focuses on quality, VFMF considers numerous factors as a way to smooth returns over time.

Holdings & Performance

Top Holdings

The two funds have very little overlap in terms of top ten holdings. Top holdings for VFQY include names such as Lam Research Corporation (LRCX), KLA Corporation (KLAC), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), and Walmart Inc. (WMT), among other popular names. In total, top ten holdings account for just 15.95% of total holdings, meaning that the fund is not overly concentrated in top positions.

For VFMF, top ten holdings include names such as Newmont Corporation (NEM), Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), Altria Group, Inc. (MO), Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), and AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), among others. Top ten holdings account for 9.18%. With even less concentration towards the top holdings than VFQY, the fund is likewise broadly diversified across positions.

Sector Allocation

In terms of sector allocation, VFQY is much more concentrated in technology, as compared to VFMF. Technology accounts for 21.64% of holdings, with financials, industrials, consumer cyclical, consumer defensive, and health care all making up a sizable portion of the fund.

VFMF’s exposure to technology is reduced (when compared to VFQY). Top sectors include financials (27.00%), healthcare (14.02%), technology (13.89%), and consumer cyclical (12.76%). Exposure to remaining sectors remains below 10%.

For VFQY, technology remains a key driver of returns. With the AI space continuing to grow, ongoing investment into AI generally supports a long-term positive outlook for higher-quality tech companies. These firms tend to be better capitalized and more profitable. However, near-term risks include exuberant valuations, rising CapEx, and potential normalization of earnings. For these reasons, while VFQY’s quality screening process tends to favor more mature tech companies, appreciation to the upside could remain capped.

As for VFMF, the fund is largely allocated towards financials. This leads to an increase in sensitivity to macroeconomic uncertainty. While I believe banks and insurers will likely continue to benefit from stable earnings and returns, forward-looking performance remains connected to interest rate moves, credit conditions, and economic growth. Therefore, negative economic conditions could weigh negatively on the sector.

As for both funds, consumer cyclicals remain a key part of total holdings. While consumer spending remains a strong driver of growth, a slowdown in consumer activity (which is common in late-cycle environments similar to what we are in now) could be a drag on the sector, creating negative headwinds for both funds.

In total, forward-looking sector exposure for VFQY and VFMF suggests limited catalysts in terms of outperformance. With structural tilts to tech and financials, investors should continue to monitor ongoing sector performance, as both funds remain sensitive to macroeconomic uncertainty.

While the current sector allocations of VFQY and VFMF provide a snapshot of current portfolio exposure, it is important to keep in mind that forward returns will depend on how key sectors evolve amid shifting macroeconomic conditions and how effectively each fund’s factor-based methodology is able to adapt to those changes. This is further supported by the low-to-moderate turnover ratios for both funds. Based on the most recent semi-annual report for VFQY, the fund maintains 413 portfolio positions with a 20% turnover ratio. Likewise, VFMF maintains 561 portfolio positions with a 31% turnover ratio. While allocations will change over time, it tends to be a more gradual change.

Recent Performance

VFQY is up 7.62%, and VFMF is up 14.93% on a YoY basis. While VFMF has outperformed the Russell 3000 Index and S&P 500 (SPY), VFQY has underperformed both the index and SPY.

Looking out further, both funds have underperformed the index since their inception. Since February 2018, VFQY has returned 103.47%, and VFMF has returned 102.38%. In comparison, the Russell 3000 has returned 247.95% while the S&P 500 has returned 256.31%.

While recent returns have more closely tracked the broader market, long-term, both funds have significantly underperformed.

Valuation

When compared to VFMF, VFQY appears to be more on the expensive side. Price/earnings, price/book, price/sales, and price/cash flow are all elevated when compared to the multifactor fund.

However, using the Russell 3000 as our benchmark, we can see that both VFQY and VFMF have favorable valuation metrics when compared to the index. Even though the funds have historically underperformed the benchmark based on price returns, valuation metrics appear favorable.

Additional Factors

As for additional factors, both VFQY and VFMF maintain strong Seeking Alpha Momentum and Expense Grades, while having weak to average Dividend grades.

VFQY has a Momentum grade of B-, with six-month, three-year, and five-year total returns all exceeding the median of all other ETFs. Likewise, with an expense ratio of 0.13%, the fund receives a Seeking Alpha Expense grade of A.

VFMF has a Momentum grade of A-. Similar to VFQY, the fund also outperforms the median of all other ETFs in terms of total returns. Also, true to Vanguard form, the fund offers a low expense ratio of 0.18% (receiving a Seeking Alpha grade of A-)

Where both funds struggle is in dividend payments. With VFQY’s dividend yield of 1.13% and VFMF’s dividend yield of 1.47%, both funds offer yields lower than that of the median of all other ETFs (2.73%).

With muted overall returns compared to the broad market and benchmark, and similarly underwhelming dividend yields, I believe income-oriented investors will find better dividend-paying opportunities elsewhere.

Quality Factor vs. Multifactor

Since the inception of both funds in February 2018, the market has seen three major selloffs resulting in a >20% decline. During these periods, VFQY and VFMF did not fare any better than the broad market, despite their quality and multifactor screening processes.

Looking at the Covid-19 crash of 2020, the inflation induced market decline of 2022, and the early 2025 tariff scare, markets declined by roughly -34%, -27%, and -21%, respectively.

During these periods, VFQY saw peak-to-trough declines of approximately -37%, -36%, and -20%. This implies that, despite the screening process for high-quality stocks, in most cases, one would have been better off just investing in the broad market.

Likewise, during these three selloff periods, VFMF saw returns of approximately -41%, -25%, and -20%. Again, suggesting that despite the rigorous multifactor screening process, one would have been better off just investing in the broad market. This is further reinforced by the fact that both the benchmark Russell 3000 and S&P 500 saw better returns following these periods when compared to VFQY and VFMF.

Risks

Risks include concentration in the technology and financial sectors, as well as geographical risk.

With the funds invested in US equities, returns are entirely dependent on US stock market performance. With tariff uncertainty continuing into 2026, as well as geopolitical and economic tensions, investors should consider VFQY and VFMF in the context of their existing portfolio allocation.

Likewise, VFQY maintains 21.64% of holdings in technology, whereas VFMF maintains 27% in financials. Investors should factor this into their investment decision, especially if they already have significant exposure to the US technology and/or financial sectors.

Additionally, given the low-to-moderate portfolio turnover, both funds could be slow to react to major economic/market changes. As noted, changes in sector leadership may not be reflected in real time, resulting in holdings that do not adequately align with existing market conditions.

Conclusion

Since inception, both VFQY and VFMF have underperformed the benchmark Russell 3000 Index and S&P 500. While valuation metrics appear favorable (compared to the benchmark), based on current sector tilts facing mixed headwinds and both funds having failed to protect against the downside during multiple recent major market selloffs, I rate VFQY and VFMF a ‘Hold’. Investors should continue to monitor structural tilts to tech and financials, as well as ongoing sector performance, as both funds remain sensitive to evolving macroeconomic conditions.
